title: Sealing a loft conversion access hatch
subtitle: Insulated and airtight hatch for a room-in-roof conversion that opens into a cold loft void
excerpt: A loft conversion often leaves a small access hatch into the remaining cold void above or beside the habitable room. This hatch is rarely insulated or draught-sealed. Treated like a mini external door, it leaks warm air directly into the unheated void. This guide covers fitting a rigid insulation board to the hatch door face and sealing the frame with compression strip.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust guidance on loft hatch insulation. gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B on insulation continuity at room-in-roof junctions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The resistance of a building envelope to uncontrolled air leakage. Measured as air changes per hour at 50 Pa pressure difference.

content.text: A loft conversion access hatch typically measures 500 x 500 mm and opens directly from a heated bedroom into a cold, uninsulated void. Without a seal, it leaks as much warm air as a 500 mm diameter hole in the wall. The fix costs 30 to 40 pounds in materials, takes two hours, and saves 40 to 60 kWh per year at current occupancy levels.
comes from the compression strip around the frame; insulation value comes from a PIR board glued to the inner face of the hatch door.
25 mm PIR rigid insulation board, cut to the internal face dimensions of the hatch door
Construction adhesive or two-part foam adhesive to bond the PIR to the hatch door
Self-adhesive foam compression strip, 10 x 3 mm, to seal the gap between the door and its frame on all four sides
Aluminium tape to seal the PIR board edges (PIR cut edges are vapour-open; tape closes them)
Remove the hatch door. Measure the interior face dimensions precisely. Cut the PIR board to fit exactly.

Apply construction adhesive to the interior face of the hatch door in a grid pattern. Press the PIR board firmly against it and hold for two minutes. Leave to cure for the time specified by the adhesive manufacturer (typically 30 to 60 minutes before reinstalling the door).
Seal the cut edges of the PIR board with aluminium tape. This closes the vapour path at the board edges and prevents cold bridging at the perimeter.

Apply self-adhesive compression strip to the rebated inner face of the hatch frame on all four sides. The strip should compress by 1 to 2 mm when the door is closed. If the frame has no rebate, apply the strip to the edge of the door itself.

Rehang the door. Check that it closes flush against the compression strip without gaps at corners. A torch on the cold side of the hatch with the lights off on the warm side shows any remaining light gaps; those gaps are air leakage paths.

For hatches that open outward into the void, bond the PIR board to the exterior face of the door instead. Apply the compression strip to the frame edge rather than the door edge. A hatch on a north-facing wall of the void also benefits from 50 mm of insulation rather than 25 mm. The thicker board is worth the extra cost for any void that drops below 5 degrees Celsius in winter.

attrs.symptom: Door does not close after adding PIR board

content.text: The PIR board has added thickness that prevents the door fitting in the frame. Reduce the board to 20 mm or trim the frame rebate to accommodate.

attrs.symptom: Condensation on the inside face of the hatch after fitting

content.text: The hatch frame is still uninsulated and is conducting cold into the frame edge. Wrap the timber frame with self-adhesive foam tape on the cold side, or install a wider PIR board that overlaps the frame by 20 mm.
